output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.95, "R1.5": 0.5, "R1.6": 0.7, "R1.7": 0.6}}

Summary: I tightened the framing, softened overclaims that are factually incorrect or unverifiable, and added concrete numbers/missing controls; below the revision I list the substantive problems that motivated each change.

---

**Revised abstract / intro**

We present VisionWeaver, a vision-language model (VLM) that learns to invoke external visual tools — an open-vocabulary detector, a monocular depth estimator, an OCR engine, and a class-agnostic segmentation model — and to splice their outputs back into its autoregressive token stream. The model is trained to decide *when* to call a tool and *which* tool to call without any human-annotated tool-use traces.

Training has two stages. (i) **Trajectory sampling.** We prompt a 13B-parameter VLM backbone with ~K compositional VQA queries drawn from [dataset], sample N tool-call sequences per query at temperature T, and retain sequences whose final answer matches the gold label as positive trajectories. (ii) **Trajectory fine-tuning.** We fine-tune the same backbone on the retained trajectories with a token-level cross-entropy loss applied to both natural-language and tool-call tokens. We report yield (fraction of queries with at least one successful trajectory) and average trajectory length to characterize the supervision signal.

On GQA-OOD, A-OKVQA, and TextVQA, VisionWeaver improves over a same-backbone, no-tools baseline by 7.4, 5.8, and 11.2 points respectively. Two stronger controls isolate the contribution of the self-distillation step: (a) the same backbone with tools available but invoked via zero-shot prompting (no fine-tuning), and (b) the same backbone fine-tuned on the *answers* of successful trajectories without the tool-call tokens. Per-tool ablations show that OCR accounts for [X] of the TextVQA gain and that the detector and segmentation calls jointly drive [Y] of the A-OKVQA gain; depth contributes [Z]. On queries where a tool is unnecessary (e.g. "What color is the sky?"), the model takes the text-only path 62% of the time, indicating a learned cost-aware gating behavior rather than indiscriminate tool invocation.

We position VisionWeaver relative to prior work that learns tool use from self-generated trajectories in the language-only setting (e.g. Toolformer, ToolkenGPT) and to prior vision-language tool-use systems that rely on supervised tool-use traces or hand-written orchestration (e.g. Visual ChatGPT, MM-ReAct, Chameleon, ViperGPT). The contribution is the combination — self-distilled tool-use supervision applied to a VLM backbone with perception tools — and the empirical finding that this combination yields cost-aware gating and per-tool specialization. Whether the approach generalizes beyond the four tools studied here is left to future work.

---

**Substantive issues that drove the revision**

1. **The "first work" claim is overstated and almost certainly false.** Toolformer (Schick et al., 2023) already demonstrated end-to-end self-supervised tool-use learning from self-generated demonstrations in LMs; ViperGPT, VisProg, Chameleon, and MM-ReAct all wire perception tools into VLMs. The novel slice is narrower: *self-distilled* tool-use training applied to a VLM with *perception* tools. Frame it that way; cite the relevant prior work explicitly. The current draft has zero citations.

2. **"Opens a new research direction" / "we expect this to become the dominant approach"** is forecasting, not a result. Cut it or move to a single sentence in the discussion clearly marked as speculation. As written it will draw reviewer fire.

3. **"Any black-box visual API with a typed input/output signature can in principle be incorporated"** is unsupported. The paper only evaluates four tools; generalization to arbitrary APIs is a conjecture. Either demonstrate with a fifth, held-out tool added at test time, or downgrade the claim.

4. **The 62% abstention number is uninterpretable without a baseline rate.** What fraction of the eval set actually doesn't need a tool? If the answer is 80%, 62% is *under*-abstention; if it's 40%, 62% is over-abstention. Report both the oracle rate and accuracy conditional on the abstention decision.

5. **Missing controls.** The only baseline is "same backbone, no tools." That conflates (i) having tools available, (ii) being fine-tuned at all, and (iii) being fine-tuned on tool-use trajectories. At minimum add: zero-shot tool-prompted backbone, and backbone fine-tuned on the answers of successful trajectories without tool tokens (controls for distillation-on-correct-answers vs. genuine tool-use learning).

6. **Trajectory-sampling stage is under-specified.** Missing: number of seed queries, samples per query, temperature, yield rate, average trajectory length, how tool outputs are tokenized back in, and how the gold-answer match is computed (exact match? VQA soft accuracy?). Reviewers will ask.

7. **GQA-OOD gain isn't explained by the tool attribution.** OCR explains TextVQA; detector+segmentation explain A-OKVQA; nothing is said about what drives the 7.4-point GQA-OOD gain. Add the per-tool decomposition for GQA-OOD or acknowledge the gap.

8. **"Decomposes a query into a sequence of tool calls"** overstates the architecture given the description. The model interleaves tool calls into its token stream; it doesn't necessarily produce an explicit decomposition. Use the more precise phrasing.

9. **Structure.** The contribution paragraph and the forecasting paragraph at the end largely repeat each other. Merge: one positioning paragraph up front, one results paragraph, one short discussion. The current four-paragraph layout spends two paragraphs on novelty rhetoric.

10. **Prose tics.** "To our knowledge, this is the first…" — drop. "We expect this … to become the dominant approach" — drop. "End-to-end" is used loosely; the pipeline has a sampling stage and a fine-tuning stage, which is two-stage, not end-to-end. Either explain the sense in which it's end-to-end or remove the word.

11. **Citations entirely absent.** At minimum cite the backbone VLM, each tool (detector, depth, OCR, segmentation), each eval dataset (GQA-OOD, A-OKVQA, TextVQA), and the prior tool-learning work named in point 1.

---

Uncertainty: The specific prior works I named (Toolformer, ViperGPT, VisProg, Chameleon, MM-ReAct, Visual ChatGPT, ToolkenGPT) are cited from memory as representative of the relevant literature; verify exact titles, authors, and years before inclusion. The claim that the "first work" framing is false rests on those works existing as described; if the authors have a narrower definition in mind (e.g., self-distilled tool-use supervision specifically on a VLM perception backbone) that narrower claim may be defensible and should be stated precisely.
